food_subgroup: Offal and organ meats — braised or stir-fried in spiced sauce

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general beef tripe food product node; no specific mala-spiced preparation exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKP covers beef stomach/tripe as a raw/generic category. No mala-spiced prepared dish code exists; closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code exists for mala-spiced beef tripe as a prepared dish. SNOMED CT does not cover this level of culinary specificity.

main_ingredients: mala spice paste (doubanjiang, dried chillies, Sichuan peppercorns)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of beef tripe mala is approximately 250 g, presented as a bowl or plate of sliced tripe coated in mala sauce with aromatics.

nutrient_source.title: Beef tripe, cooked — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy (NDB 13351); mala sauce macros estimated from component analysis using USDA Foundation Foods for Sichuan spice paste and oil components
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base tripe macros from USDA SR Legacy NDB 13351 (beef tripe, cooked, simmered). Mala sauce contribution (oil, chilli paste, soy sauce) estimated from USDA Foundation Foods component data and blended proportionally at approximately 20% sauce by weight. Final per-100g values represent a composite of ~80% cooked tripe and ~20% mala sauce. Sodium elevated to reflect soy sauce and doubanjiang content typical of mala preparations.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat and offal dishes — analogous estimate based on cooked beef offal; Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database; Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for beef tripe mala. GI estimated at 25 based on analogy with cooked beef offal and high-protein, low-carbohydrate meat dishes (GI range 20–30) from the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 international GI tables. Carbohydrate content is low (~3.5g/100g), so glycemic load per 250g serving = (25 × 3.5 × 2.5) / 100 = 2.2, confirming negligible glycemic impact.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content from soy sauce and doubanjiang in mala sauce (~620 mg sodium per 100g; ~1550 mg per 250g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid beef tripe mala due to its very high sodium content (~1550 mg per serving, exceeding 67% of the 2300 mg daily limit), and should request reduced-sauce or sauce-on-side preparation if consumed.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low carbohydrate content (~3.5g/100g) and low glycemic index (~25) result in negligible glycemic load per serving (~2.2)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Beef tripe mala has a low glycemic index and very low carbohydrate content, making it unlikely to cause significant postprandial glucose elevation; however, individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should monitor total meal carbohydrate load and be aware of the high sodium content if hypertension is a comorbidity.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat (~2.4g/100g), elevated cholesterol (~95mg/100g), and high sodium (~620mg/100g) are relevant cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume beef tripe mala in moderation given its cholesterol and saturated fat content, and should be particularly cautious of the high sodium load from mala sauce, which may exacerbate blood pressure and fluid retention.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Beef Tripe Mala (麻辣牛肚, Má Là Niú Dǔ) is a Chinese Sichuan-style dish featuring sliced beef tripe — typically honeycomb or smooth tripe — cooked in or tossed with a mala (麻辣, meaning 'numbing and spicy') sauce. The mala sauce is built from doubanjiang (fermented broad bean and chilli paste), dried red chillies, Sichuan peppercorns (which produce the characteristic mouth-numbing sensation), garlic, ginger, soy sauce, and sesame oil. The dish is popular across Chinese-speaking communities in Singapore and Southeast Asia, often served as a main course or sharing dish. Texturally, beef tripe is chewy and slightly gelatinous, absorbing the bold, oily, and intensely spiced sauce. Nutritionally, beef tripe is a high-protein, moderate-fat, very low-carbohydrate food with notable vitamin B12 content (~1.2 mcg/100g) and iron (~1.4 mg/100g), making it nutritionally dense for its calorie count (~148 kcal/100g). The mala sauce contributes significant sodium (~620 mg/100g), which is the primary clinical concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 25 (low), and the glycemic load per standard 250g serving is approximately 2.2, making it suitable for individuals managing blood glucose. The dish is not suitable for vegetarians, vegans, or those observing kosher dietary laws, and halal status depends on the source of the beef and preparation method. In Singapore, this dish is commonly found at Chinese zi char restaurants, mala xiang guo stalls, and cafes offering Chinese-style comfort food.
common_user_queries: How many calories in beef tripe mala?
Is beef tripe mala high in sodium?
Can diabetics eat beef tripe mala?
What is mala sauce made of?
What does beef tripe taste like?
How is mala beef tripe prepared?
What is the GI of beef tripe?

confidence_reason: Base tripe macros sourced from USDA SR Legacy (cooked beef tripe, NDB 13351), a well-validated approved source. Mala sauce contribution estimated from USDA Foundation Foods component data. No exact match for mala-spiced beef tripe exists in any primary regional database (HPB Singapore Foodo, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD). GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach for the sauce component.
notes: 1. Cuisine tag in input JSON was 'Italian' — this is clearly erroneous metadata from the field survey restaurant category (The Light Cafe lists Italian/Pizza/Coffee/Toast tags). The food itself is a Chinese Sichuan-style dish; cuisine corrected to 'Chinese (Sichuan)'. 2. Nutrient values are a composite estimate: ~80% cooked beef tripe (USDA SR Legacy NDB 13351) and ~20% mala sauce (estimated from USDA Foundation Foods components). Sodium is elevated to reflect typical mala sauce sodium load from soy sauce and doubanjiang. 3. GI of 25 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for beef tripe mala exists in the University of Sydney GI Database or peer-reviewed literature as of April 2026. 4. Halal status is marked false as the halal certification of the specific restaurant (The Light Cafe — High Street Plaza) and beef source cannot be confirmed from available data. 5. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy entry for cooked beef tripe (NDB 13351) and is not estimable from the sauce component. 6. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are estimated from USDA SR Legacy NDB 13351 with proportional adjustment for sauce component. 7. The Burmese script in the original food_name (အမဲအူကြမ်းမာလာ) indicates this dish is also known in Burmese; however, Burmese is not a mandatory language for this cuisine category and has been noted in aliases context only.
